claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand a bit about your child or children.

**How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can check each of them)

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you'd like, their name so I can keep track as we go through the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children - Alex (17 years old) and Blake (3 years old). I'll help you assess eligibility for both of them.

Let's start with **Blake, who is 3 years old**. Since Blake is under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. However, I need to check one important thing:

**Is Blake working full-time (meaning 24 hours or more per week)?**

This would be very unusual for a 3-year-old, but I need to ask this question as part of the official assessment process.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - Blake is only 3! They're not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That makes sense. Now let me check a few more things for Blake.

**Is Blake currently in prison or a young offenders institution?**

(Again, this would be very unusual for a 3-year-old, but it's part of the standard checks I need to go through.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, absolutely not. Blake is just a toddler - they're not in prison or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now I need to ask about your living situation with Blake:

**Do you live with Blake in the UK, or does Blake live with you in the UK?**

This is an important residency requirement for Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives with me here in the UK. We're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check an important aspect about your residency status and rights in the UK. This determines if you meet the requirements to claim Child Benefit.

**Do any of the following apply to you?**

1. You are ordinarily resident in the UK
2. You have Right to Reside in the UK
3. You are a Crown servant (or their partner) posted overseas
4. You are a member of HM Forces (or their partner)
5. You work for an EU institution or international organization with special status
6. You are a frontier worker or cross-border worker
7. You are subject to immigration control but have specific benefits access
8. You are a person with special circumstances (e.g., refugee, asylum seeker with benefits access)

In most cases, if you're living and working in the UK normally, you would meet these requirements through being "ordinarily resident" or having "Right to Reside."

**Do any of these apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live here ordinarily - I'm a UK resident living in Manchester with my children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check one more important question for Blake:

**Is there anyone else also claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child. If multiple people try to claim for the same child, HMRC needs to decide who has priority.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's all the information I need for Blake.

Now let's move on to **Alex, who is 17 years old**. Since Alex is between 16 and 19, their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

This typically includes:
- Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs
- Education at school or college (not university degree courses)

Is Alex in any of these types of education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in education at the moment. They left school and aren't studying or doing any training courses right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17 and not currently in approved education or training, I need to check if there's a specific extension period that might apply.

**Did Alex recently leave approved education or training within the last few months?**

There can be an extension period that covers the time between when a young person leaves education/training and a certain date. If Alex recently left education, they might still be covered for a short period.

When did Alex leave school or education?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They left school a while back - it's been several months now. They haven't signed up with the careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Let me check one more thing about Alex's situation:

**Is Alex currently registered with the careers service (or an approved body) and waiting to start approved education or training?**

This is another way that 16-19 year olds can qualify - if they're registered and waiting to start a course or training program.

You mentioned they haven't signed up with the careers service, so I'm assuming the answer is no, but I want to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - no, Alex hasn't registered with the careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming. Now I need to check a few standard questions for Alex, similar to what I asked about Blake:

**Is Alex working full-time (24 hours or more per week)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working full-time. They're not working at all at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. A couple more quick checks for Alex:

**Is Alex currently in prison or a young offenders institution?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in prison or a young offenders institution.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. Now, similar to what I asked about Blake:

**Does Alex live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me here at home in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. And the same residency question applies - you've already confirmed you're ordinarily resident in the UK, so that covers both children.

One final check for Alex:

**Is there anyone else also claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess the eligibility for both of your children based on what you've told me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

---

### **Blake (age 3): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake. 

Blake qualifies because:
- Blake is under 16 years old
- Blake is not working full-time
- Blake lives with you in the UK
- You are ordinarily resident in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake

---

### **Alex (age 17): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you are not currently eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

The reason is that Alex is 17 years old, which falls into the 16-19 age bracket. For children aged 16-19, they must be in **approved education or training** to qualify for Child Benefit. Since Alex:
- Is not currently in approved education or training
- Left school several months ago (so no extension period applies)
- Is not registered with the careers service waiting to start education/training

Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria at this time.

---

### **What This Means for You**

You can claim Child Benefit for Blake, but not for Alex in their current situation.

### **Important Information About Alex**

If Alex's situation changes, you may become eligible to claim for them. Alex could qualify again if they:
- Enroll in approved full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, BTECs, NVQs Level 3 or below)
- Start an approved training program
- Register with the careers service while waiting to start education or training

If any of these situations occur, you can contact HMRC to update your claim.

---

### **Next Steps**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit for Blake** if you haven't already done so
2. **Keep HMRC updated** if Alex returns to education or training
3. You can apply online or by post using form CH2

Would you like any more information about making your claim or anything else about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Blake is 3 years old (under 16), not working full-time, not in prison/young offenders institution, lives with the claimant in the UK, the claimant is ordinarily resident in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Blake meets all the qualifying child criteria for Child Benefit.

Alex is 17 years old (between 16-19), which requires being in approved education or training to qualify. Alex is not currently in approved education or training, did not recently leave education (left several months ago so no extension period applies), is not registered with careers service waiting to start education/training, is not working full-time, is not in prison/young offenders institution, and lives with the claimant in the UK. However, because Alex is not in approved education or training and does not meet any of the extension criteria, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Blake (3) and Alex (17). The claimant is ordinarily resident in the UK and lives with both children in Manchester. Blake qualifies for Child Benefit as a child under 16 who meets all standard requirements. Alex does not qualify because, although aged 16-19, Alex is not in approved education or training and does not meet any extension criteria. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake only.
